title: Hello Java
subtitle: Meet the language that runs on billions of devices — phones, games, banks, and more.
goal: Understand what Java is and the JVM, write a complete Hello-World program, and use System.out.println and comments.

blocks.heading: What is Java? ☕
blocks.html:
Java is a programming language — a way to give a computer step-by-step instructions. It powers Android apps, huge websites, video games like Minecraft, and the software banks use to move money safely.

Java has been around for nearly 30 years and is still one of the most popular languages on Earth. Learn it well, and you can build almost anything.

blocks.heading: Write once, run anywhere: the JVM
blocks.html:
Here's Java's superpower. When you write Java, it gets turned into a special middle-language called bytecode. That bytecode runs on something called the JVM — the Java Virtual Machine.

Every kind of computer (Windows, Mac, phone, fridge…) has its own JVM. So the same Java program runs everywhere without changes. People call this “write once, run anywhere.” 🌍

Java runs on the JVM, so it can't run for free inside a web browser like some languages can. On this site you'll read the code, predict what it prints, then reveal the real answer — a skill real programmers use every day.

blocks.heading: Every Java program needs a class and a main
blocks.html:
Java is tidy: all code lives inside a class. To start a program, Java looks for one special method called main. The computer always begins running there.

The opening line looks scary at first, but you'll type it so often it becomes second nature:

public class Main { public static void main(String[] args) { ... } }

blocks.caption: Your first Java program — read it, predict the output 👇
blocks.explain: System.out.println(...) prints whatever is inside the parentheses and then moves to a new line. The text goes in double quotes "..." — we call text a 'String'. Notice every statement ends with a semicolon ;

blocks.html: Semicolons matter! In Java, almost every statement ends with a ;. Forget one and Java refuses to run. Think of it like the period at the end of a sentence.

blocks.caption: You can print as many lines as you like
blocks.output:
I am learning Java.
This is line two!
Java is fun. ☕
blocks.explain: Each println makes its own new line, and Java runs your statements top to bottom, in order.

blocks.heading: Comments: notes for humans 📝
blocks.html:
A comment is a note the computer ignores. Use // for a single line, or /* ... */ to span several lines. Comments help you (and others) remember why the code does what it does.

Also meet System.out.print — just like println but it does not add a new line afterward.

blocks.caption: Comments are skipped; print vs println
blocks.output:
Java ignores the comment lines
No new line here. Same line!
blocks.explain: The // and /* */ parts never print. And because the first one used print (not println), the next text stayed on the same line.

blocks.heading: How a Java program actually runs
blocks.html:
Behind the scenes there are two steps: compile (turn your .java code into bytecode) and run (the JVM executes that bytecode). The classic tools are javac Main.java then java Main.

Modern Java (version 11+) lets you skip a step for single files: just run java Main.java and it compiles and runs in one go — handy while learning.

blocks.question: What does System.out.println("Hi") do?
options: Prints the word Hi and moves to a new line
blocks.explain: println prints whatever is inside the quotes — here, the text Hi — then starts a fresh line.

blocks.question: What is the JVM?
options: A website where Java code lives
The Java Virtual Machine that runs Java bytecode on any computer
blocks.explain: The JVM (Java Virtual Machine) runs the bytecode, which is why the same Java program works on many kinds of computers — write once, run anywhere.

blocks.question: Why does Java need a semicolon at the end of System.out.println("Hi")?
options: It tells Java that statement is finished
blocks.explain: A semicolon ends a statement in Java, like a period ends a sentence. Leave it out and the program won't compile.

blocks.question: Where does a Java program start running?
options: At the very first line of the file
Inside the special method called main
It starts at the bottom and goes up
blocks.explain: Java always begins at the main method inside your class. That's the front door — the computer steps inside there first and runs your code from top to bottom.

blocks.title: Print your own name
blocks.instructions: Inside main, change the text so the program prints YOUR name. Keep the full class and main structure. Then press Check.
blocks.passMsg: Awesome — you just wrote a real Java program!
blocks.failMsg: Keep System.out.println("...") and put your name inside the quotes after 'My name is'.

blocks.title: Three facts about you
blocks.instructions: Use three println statements inside main to print three fun facts about yourself — your age, favorite food, favorite color, anything! Each on its own line.
blocks.solutionOutput:
I am 12 years old.
I love pizza.
My favorite color is blue.
blocks.passMsg: Three lines, three facts — nicely done!
blocks.failMsg: Add at least three System.out.println lines, each printing one fact.

challenge.title: Make an ASCII greeting card
challenge.instructions: Use println statements inside main to draw a little greeting on screen: a top border line, a message in the middle, and a matching bottom border. Make it say hello to someone you like!
challenge.hint: You need at least three println lines: a border, your message, and another border. Keep them the same width so it looks neat.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Beautiful card! You've finished Day 1 — you're officially a Java programmer now.
